Triggers on: "generate tests", "write tests", "add tests", "create test", "test this". user-invocable: true argument-hint: "[file or function] [--framework jest|vitest|pytest|playwright] [--type unit|integration|e2e]" allowed-tools: "Read, Write, Edit, Grep, Glob, Bash(npm test*), Bash(npm run*), Bash(npx vitest*), Bash(npx jest*), Bash(npx playwright*), Bash(npx stryker*), Bash(pytest*), Bash(python -m pytest*), Bash(cargo test*), Bash(go test*), Bash(mvn test*), Bash(./gradlew test*)" genskills-version: "1.5.0" genskills-category: "code-quality" genskills-depends: [] --- # Test Generator Generate comprehensive, well-structured, deterministic test suites that document behavior and catch regressions - across Jest, Vitest, pytest, Go testing, JUnit, and more. ## Core Principles Internalize these before writing a single assertion: 1. **Test behavior, not implementation.** Assert on observable outcomes (return values, persisted records, rendered output, emitted events) - never on private internals. A test that breaks when you refactor without changing behavior is a liability, not an asset. 2. **Arrange-Act-Assert.** Every test has one setup, one action, and one logical assertion. More than one "Act" means you are testing more than one thing - split it. 3. **Deterministic and isolated.** A test must produce the same result every run, in any order, on any machine. Fake the clock, seed randomness, stub the network, reset state in teardown. No flakiness tolerated. 4. **Meaningful coverage over a coverage number.** 100% line coverage that never asserts anything proves nothing. Cover the branches, edge cases, and error paths that matter; justify what you deliberately leave uncovered. 5. **Fast and cheap to run.** Unit tests should run in milliseconds. Push slow dependencies (real DB, real network) up the pyramid to fewer integration/e2e tests. A suite developers won't run is a suite that doesn't protect them. 6. **One reason to fail.** When a test fails, its name and single focus should point straight at what broke. Vague mega-tests waste debugging time. --- ## Process ### Step 0: Load Project Context

Triggers on: "fix CI", "CI failing", "pipeline broken", "build failed", "GitHub Actions failing". user-invocable: true argument-hint: "[run URL | run ID | 'latest']" allowed-tools: "Read, Edit, Grep, Glob, Bash(gh run *), Bash(gh api *), Bash(gh workflow *), Bash(git log*), Bash(git diff*), Bash(git status*), Bash(npm *), Bash(npx *), Bash(yarn *), Bash(pnpm *), Bash(pytest *), Bash(python *), Bash(go *), Bash(make *), Bash(docker *)" genskills-version: "1.5.0" genskills-category: "workflow" genskills-depends: [] --- # CI Fix A senior engineer's playbook for turning a red pipeline green - by reproducing the failure locally, reading the log the way the runner actually executed it, and fixing the root cause instead of muting the symptom. ## Core Principles Before you touch a workflow file, internalize these: 1. **Reproduce locally before you fix.** A CI failure you cannot reproduce on your machine is a guess. Recreate the runner's environment (versions, env vars, clean checkout) and run the exact failing command. A fix that only "passes CI" on retry is not a fix. 2. **Read the log bottom-up.** The first error line in a long log is rarely the real cause - it is often a downstream cascade. Start at the failing step's exit, find the first non-zero exit code, then walk upward to the originating error. 3. **Distinguish flaky from real.** Before fixing "the test," check whether the same commit passes on rerun. A test that fails non-deterministically is a flakiness bug (race, ordering, network, time) - retrying it hides the defect. A test that fails every time on this commit is a real regression. 4. **Fix the root cause, not the symptom.** Bumping a timeout, adding `continue-on-error`, pinning to an old runner, or retrying a flaky test are symptom patches. Find why the step failed - then the patch becomes unnecessary. 5. **CI is just a clean machine.** Most "works on my machine" failures are environment drift: a globally installed tool, a stale local cache, an uncommitted file, or an env var set in your shell. The runner has none of that. Treat divergence from a clean checkout as the prime suspect. 6. **Change one thing, then re-run.** CI round-trips are slow. Resist shotgun-debugging across five commits. Form one hypothesis, make the minimal change, verify locally, then push. --- ## Process ### Step 0: Load Project Context

Triggers on: "deploy", "ship to production", "release", "roll out", "promote to prod". user-invocable: true disable-model-invocation: true argument-hint: "[environment: staging|production] [strategy: rolling|canary|blue-green]" allowed-tools: "Read, Grep, Glob, Bash(git status), Bash(git fetch*), Bash(git log*), Bash(git rev-parse*), Bash(git tag*), Bash(npm run *), Bash(npm test*), Bash(npm audit*), Bash(npm ci*), Bash(npx *), Bash(pnpm *), Bash(yarn *), Bash(pip audit*), Bash(vercel *), Bash(netlify *), Bash(fly *), Bash(flyctl *), Bash(docker *), Bash(kubectl *), Bash(helm *), Bash(aws *), Bash(gcloud *), Bash(terraform *), Bash(gh *), Bash(curl *)" genskills-version: "1.5.0" genskills-category: "workflow" genskills-depends: [] --- # Deploy A release engineer's playbook for shipping code safely - from a single Vercel preview to a multi-region canary rollout on Kubernetes. The goal is not just to deploy, but to deploy in a way that is reversible, observable, and low-blast-radius. **Note**: This skill is user-invocable only (not auto-triggered) for safety. Every command that mutates a remote environment requires explicit user confirmation. Production deploys require double confirmation. ## Core Principles Internalize these before touching any environment: 1. **Every deploy must be reversible.** Know the rollback command *before* you ship. A deploy you cannot undo is a gamble, not a release. Capture the current version/revision/digest first. 2. **Verify before you promote.** A green CI build is necessary but not sufficient. Smoke-test the artifact in a staging environment that mirrors production before promoting it. 3. **Minimize blast radius.** Prefer strategies that expose a new version to a fraction of traffic first (canary, blue-green) over flipping 100% of traffic at once. A bug should hurt 1% of users, not all of them. 4. **Zero-downtime by default.** Use rolling updates, health-gated readiness, and connection draining so users never see an error during a release. Never `stop && start`. 5. **Production is sacred.** Confirm explicitly, deploy from the right branch/tag, and never deploy on Friday afternoon or during an active incident unless the deploy *is* the fix. 6. **Observe the release, don't fire-and-forget.** A deploy is not "done" when the CLI exits 0. It is done when error rates, latency, and saturation are confirmed nominal in the post-deploy window. --- ## Process ### Step 0: Load Project Context

skills.name: genskills--github-actions
skills.description: Generate and fix GitHub Actions workflows - CI/CD, matrix builds, reusable workflows, composite actions, OIDC cloud auth, security hardening. Triggers on: "github actions", "github workflow", "CI pipeline", "CD pipeline", "create workflow", "actions yaml". user-invocable: true argument-hint: "[type] - e.g., 'ci' or 'deploy' or 'release' or 'full'" allowed-tools: "Read, Write, Edit, Grep, Glob, Bash(gh *), Bash(git *), Bash(npm *), Bash(act *), Bash(actionlint *), Bash(yamllint *)" genskills-version: "1.5.0" genskills-category: "devops" genskills-depends: [] --- # GitHub Actions A CI/CD engineer's playbook for authoring, hardening, and debugging GitHub Actions workflows - from a first CI run to multi-environment deployments with OIDC cloud auth and reusable workflow libraries. ## Core Principles Internalize these before writing a single line of YAML: 1. **Pin actions to a full commit SHA.** Tags and branches are mutable; a compromised tag re-point is a supply-chain attack. Pin `uses:` to the 40-char SHA and annotate with the human tag (`@b4ffde6... # v4.1.1`). Use Dependabot to bump pins. 2. **Least-privilege `GITHUB_TOKEN`.** Default the repo/workflow to `permissions: {}` (deny all), then grant the narrowest scope each job needs (`contents: read`, `id-token: write`, etc.). Never leave the implicit read/write default in place. 3. **Cache aggressively, invalidate correctly.** Cache dependencies keyed on the lockfile hash. A cache that never invalidates is a correctness bug; a cache that never hits is wasted minutes. 4. **Fail fast, surface everything.** Set `timeout-minutes` on every job so hung runs do not burn an hour. Use `concurrency` to cancel superseded runs, but set `fail-fast: false` on matrices so one leg's failure does not hide the others. 5. **No secrets in logs, ever.** Pass secrets via `secrets`/`env`, never interpolate them into `run:` strings that get echoed. Untrusted input (PR titles, branch names, issue bodies) must never be interpolated directly into a `run:` shell line - it is a script-injection vector. Assign to an `env:` var and reference `"$VAR"` instead. 6. **Prefer OIDC over long-lived secrets.** For cloud deploys (AWS/GCP/Azure) and npm publishing, use short-lived OIDC tokens (`id-token: write`) instead of storing static credentials as repo secrets. --- ## Process ### Step 0: Load Context

Triggers on: "manage branches", "clean branches", "create branch", "merge branch", "rebase", "branch cleanup". user-invocable: true argument-hint: "[action: create|switch|merge|rebase|cleanup|status] [branch or base]" allowed-tools: "Bash(git status*), Bash(git branch*), Bash(git checkout*), Bash(git switch*), Bash(git fetch*), Bash(git pull*), Bash(git merge*), Bash(git rebase*), Bash(git log*), Bash(git for-each-ref*), Bash(git symbolic-ref*), Bash(git remote*), Bash(git push*), Bash(git config*), Bash(gh pr*), Bash(gh repo*), Bash(gh api*), Read, Grep, Glob" genskills-version: "1.5.0" genskills-category: "workflow" genskills-depends: [] --- # Branch Manager A disciplined git branch operator's playbook - create, switch, merge, rebase, and prune branches without rewriting shared history, orphaning work, or breaking the default branch. ## Core Principles Internalize these before running a single git command: 1. **Never force-push a shared branch.** A branch is shared the moment it has remote tracking *and* anyone else (or an open PR) may have it checked out. Rewriting its history breaks everyone downstream. Force-push only personal, unshared branches - and even then prefer `--force-with-lease` over `--force`. 2. **The default branch is sacred.** `main`/`master` is never rebased, never force-pushed, never committed to directly when a PR workflow exists. Treat it as append-only via merges/PRs. 3. **Keep history clean and intentional.** Rebase personal feature branches onto the latest base to keep a linear, reviewable history; merge when integrating shared work. Choose deliberately - do not mix strategies mid-branch. 4. **Confirm before anything destructive.** Branch deletion, force-push, and history rewrites require explicit user confirmation. Prefer safe variants first (`git branch -d` before `-D`, `--force-with-lease` before `--force`). 5. **Naming is a contract.** Follow a consistent `<type>/<short-description>` convention so branches are self-documenting and tooling (CI, PR automation) can reason about them. 6. **Fetch before you reason.** Local refs go stale fast. `git fetch --prune` before assessing merged/stale/gone branches so decisions reflect remote reality, not a cached view. --- ## Process ### Step 0: Load Project Context

Triggers on: "commit", "save changes", "git commit", "stage and commit", "make a commit", "split commits", "amend commit". user-invocable: true argument-hint: "[optional commit message or scope hint]" allowed-tools: "Read, Grep, Glob, Bash(git *)" genskills-version: "1.5.0" genskills-category: "workflow" genskills-depends: [] --- # Git Commit A senior engineer's playbook for crafting commits that are atomic, well-described, and safe to push - from a one-line fix to a multi-commit feature split. ## Core Principles Internalize these before staging a single line: 1. **A commit is a unit of meaning, not a unit of time.** Each commit should represent one logical change that can be understood, reviewed, and reverted in isolation. "Save my work" is not a reason to commit ten unrelated edits together. 2. **The diff says what; the message says why.** The reader can already see the code change. Your job is to explain the intent, the trade-off, and the context that the diff cannot convey. 3. **Match the house style, do not impose your own.** Read the last 20 commits before writing one. A repo that uses `feat(api):` and one that uses `Fix login bug` deserve consistency, not your personal preference. 4. **Never stage blindly.** `git add -A` and `git add .` are how secrets, build artifacts, and debug `console.log`s leak into history. Stage explicit paths and read what you are committing. 5. **Hooks are guardrails, not obstacles.** `--no-verify` defeats the purpose of pre-commit checks. If a hook fails, fix the cause - the hook caught a real problem. 6. **History is append-only once shared.** Amend and rebase freely on local-only commits; never rewrite a commit that has been pushed to a shared branch. --- ## Process ### Step 0: Load Project Context

Triggers on: "which packages changed", "run tests for affected", "add workspace", "monorepo", "workspace", "turbo", "nx", "changeset". user-invocable: true argument-hint: "[action: affected|add|deps|graph|version|cache] [package name]" allowed-tools: "Read, Write, Edit, Grep, Glob, Bash(npx turbo*), Bash(turbo*), Bash(npx nx*), Bash(nx*), Bash(npx lerna*), Bash(lerna*), Bash(npx changeset*), Bash(pnpm*), Bash(npm*), Bash(npm run*), Bash(yarn*), Bash(yarn workspaces*), Bash(bazel*), Bash(git diff*), Bash(git log*), Bash(git merge-base*)" genskills-version: "1.5.0" genskills-category: "workflow" genskills-depends: [] --- # Monorepo Management A staff engineer's playbook for operating a multi-package repository: detecting what changed, building only what's affected, keeping the dependency graph clean, and shipping versioned packages without manual bookkeeping. ## Core Principles Internalize these before touching the workspace: 1. **Single source of truth.** One lockfile, one version policy, one shared toolchain config. Every package inherits from the root; nothing is configured twice. Drift between packages is technical debt that compounds with every new package. 2. **Affected-only by default.** Never build, test, or lint the whole repo when a three-line change touched one leaf package. Compute the affected set from the merge-base and operate on that closure only. Whole-repo runs are reserved for dependency-graph or root-config changes. 3. **The dependency graph is acyclic and explicit.** Inter-package dependencies are declared in `package.json`, never reached through relative `../../other-package` imports. Cycles are always bugs - they break topological build order, caching, and incremental publishing. 4. **Consistent tooling everywhere.** TypeScript, ESLint, Prettier, and test config live at the root and are extended, not copied. A contributor moving between packages should never relearn the toolchain. 5. **Cache aggressively, invalidate precisely.** Task outputs are pure functions of their inputs (source + deps + config + env). Declare those inputs accurately so the cache is correct, then let local and remote caches eliminate redundant work. 6. **Versioning is a graph operation, not a manual edit.** When a package changes, every dependent that ships it must bump too. Use changesets (or the tool's native release flow) so version bumps and changelogs propagate through the graph automatically. --- ## Process ### Step 0: Load Project Context

You roll *forward* with a new version. A release you cannot recover from is a release you should not cut. 6. **Confirm at the irreversible boundary.** Local bumps and commits are cheap and revertible; pushing tags and publishing to registries are not. Always pause for explicit human confirmation at that boundary unless `autoPublish` is set. --- ## Process ### Step 0: Load Context

Triggers on: "review this code", "code review", "check my code", "review PR", "review changes". user-invocable: true argument-hint: "[file or directory] [--mode quick|deep|security] [--pr <number>]" allowed-tools: "Read, Edit, Grep, Glob, WebFetch, Bash(git diff*), Bash(git log*), Bash(git blame*), Bash(git show*), Bash(npm test*), Bash(npm run*), Bash(npx vitest*), Bash(npx jest*), Bash(gh pr*)" genskills-version: "1.5.0" genskills-category: "code-quality" genskills-depends: [] --- # Code Review A senior reviewer's playbook for a thorough, multi-dimensional code review - catching the bug that ships to production, the security hole that leaks data, and the abstraction that costs the team six months from now. ## Core Principles Internalize these before you read a single line of the diff: 1. **Review the change, judge against the whole.** Read the diff to know what changed, but read the surrounding code to know whether it's correct. A line that looks fine in isolation can be wrong in context. 2. **Severity is about impact, not taste.** A finding's level reflects what breaks if it ships - not how much you dislike the style. State the failure mode, not the rule. 3. **Every finding is actionable.** "This is bad" is noise. "This is bad, here's why it matters, here's the fix" is a review. Anchor each item to `file:line`. 4. **Defer to the tools for what tools can see.** Formatting, import order, and lint rules belong to linters and formatters. Spend human attention on logic, security, and design - the things automation misses. 5. **Praise is part of the job.** Reinforcing good patterns scales quality across a team faster than catching bad ones. Call out what was done well. 6. **Calibrate confidence and say so.** Distinguish "I traced this and it's broken" from "this pattern often hides a bug." Labeling uncertainty earns trust and helps the author triage. --- ## Review Process ### Step 0: Load Project Context

Triggers on: "dead code", "unused code", "find unused", "remove dead code", "orphan files", "unused exports". user-invocable: true argument-hint: "[scope] - e.g., 'src/' or 'full' or 'exports-only'" allowed-tools: "Read, Edit, Grep, Glob, Bash(npx ts-prune*), Bash(npx knip*), Bash(npx unimported*), Bash(npx depcheck*), Bash(npx eslint*), Bash(npm *), Bash(pnpm *), Bash(yarn *), Bash(vulture *), Bash(python *), Bash(deadcode *), Bash(go *), Bash(git log*), Bash(git rm*)" genskills-version: "1.5.0" genskills-category: "code-quality" genskills-depends: [] --- # Dead Code A disciplined playbook for finding and **safely** removing dead code - unused exports, unreachable branches, unused variables, orphan files, stale dependencies, and abandoned feature flags. Deleting code is one of the highest-value, lowest-risk improvements you can make to a codebase *if* you prove the code is truly dead first. This skill is built around that proof. ## Core Principles Internalize these before deleting a single line: 1. **Prove it's dead before you delete it.** "I can't find a reference" is a hypothesis, not a proof. Confirm with at least two independent signals (e.g., a tool report *and* a manual `Grep`, or zero coverage *and* no string-based references) before removal. 2. **Dynamic and reflective usage is invisible to static analysis.** Code reached via `import()`, `require(variable)`, reflection, decorators, DI containers, string-keyed registries, or framework conventions will look unused but isn't. Always check for these before trusting a "0 references" result. 3. **A public API contract is not dead code.** Anything exported from a published package, exposed over a network boundary, or consumed by code you cannot see is load-bearing regardless of internal reference count. Treat package entry points and SDK surfaces as live. 4. **Delete in small, reviewable, reversible steps.** One category or one module per commit. Large "remove all dead code" diffs hide mistakes and are impossible to bisect. Every removal must be revertible in isolation. 5. **Tests and tree-shaking are your evidence, not just your safety net.** A green test suite after removal is corroborating evidence that the code was dead. A bundler that tree-shakes the symbol away proves the production build never needed it. Use both as confirmation signals. 6. **When in doubt, deprecate - don't delete.** If confidence is low and the cost of a wrong deletion is high (public API, rarely-exercised error path), mark it deprecated and remove it in a later cycle once telemetry or a full release confirms it's unused. --- ## Process ### Step 0: Load Context

The cheapest vulnerability is the one in a package you removed. Prefer the standard library, then a small focused package, then a framework - never the reverse. 2. **Audit before you upgrade.** Understand *why* a package is present, *what* pulls it in, and *what changed* before bumping a version. A blind `npm update` trades a known state for an unknown one. 3. **The lockfile is the truth.** `package.json` ranges describe intent; the lockfile describes reality. Audit, reproduce, and reason about the resolved versions in the lockfile - not the caret ranges in the manifest. 4. **Watch the transitive tree.** Most of your dependency weight, CVEs, and license exposure lives in packages you never named. A clean top level over a rotten transitive tree is a false sense of safety. 5. **Treat the registry as hostile.** Typosquats, hijacked maintainer accounts, and malicious post-install scripts are real and routine. New, recently-republished, or low-trust packages deserve scrutiny before they ever run on your machine or in CI. 6. **Upgrade in safe, reversible increments.** One concern per change, lowest risk first (security patches, then patch/minor, then majors one at a time), each verified by the test suite and revertable via a single commit. --- ## Process ### Step 0: Load Project Context

Triggers on: "find unhandled errors", "error coverage", "error boundaries", "unhandled exceptions", "missing error handling". user-invocable: true argument-hint: "[file or directory] [--fix] [--severity critical|high|medium|low]" allowed-tools: "Read, Write, Edit, Grep, Glob, Bash(npm test*), Bash(npm run*), Bash(npx *), Bash(yarn *), Bash(pnpm *), Bash(git diff*), Bash(git log*)" genskills-version: "1.5.0" genskills-category: "code-quality" genskills-depends: [] --- # Error Boundary Analysis A senior engineer's playbook for finding the places where your code fails loudly to the user, silently to nobody, or fatally to the process - and for installing the right boundary, at the right granularity, that logs, reports, and recovers. ## Core Principles Before you add a single `catch`, internalize these: 1. **Fail gracefully, never silently.** Every error surface needs a defined outcome: a safe response, a fallback UI, a retry, or a deliberate crash-and-restart. The worst outcome is an error that disappears - the user sees a blank screen, the process stays in a corrupt state, and nobody is paged. 2. **Never swallow errors.** An empty `catch {}` or a `catch` that returns a default value (`[]`, `null`, `200 OK`) converts a loud, fixable failure into a silent data bug that surfaces weeks later. If you catch it, you must log it, report it, re-throw it, or convert it into an explicit handled state. 3. **Place boundaries at the right granularity.** Match the boundary to the blast radius. One root boundary means any error blanks the whole app; a boundary per independently-failing region means one widget degrades while the rest of the page keeps working. Too coarse hides failures; too fine is noise. 4. **Log, report, recover - in that order.** When a boundary fires: log it once (not in every layer), report it once to your monitoring service with enough context to correlate (request id, digest, user/tenant), then recover - render a fallback, return a safe response, or restart the process. 5. **The client must never see internals.** Stack traces and raw error messages leak architecture and secrets, and confuse users. In production, return a generic message plus a correlation id; keep the detail in the logs. 6. **Distinguish recoverable from fatal.** A failed fetch is recoverable - retry or show a fallback. A corrupt process state (unhandled rejection, uncaught exception) is fatal - log, flush telemetry, and exit so the supervisor restarts cleanly. Treating fatal as recoverable leaves a zombie process serving wrong answers. --- ## Process ### Step 0: Load Project Context

Budget it at under ~5 seconds. Operate on **staged files only** (lint-staged / `{staged_files}`), never the whole tree. Slow hooks train developers to reach for `--no-verify`. 2. **Right check, right stage.** Cheap, file-local checks (lint, format, secret scan) go in **pre-commit**. Expensive, whole-project checks (type-check, full test suite, build) go in **pre-push** where they run once per push, not once per commit. 3. **Fail loud, fail clear.** A hook that fails must exit non-zero with a message that says *what* failed and *how to fix or bypass it*. Silent or cryptic failures erode trust in the whole hook layer. 4. **Hooks are local, not a security boundary.** Anyone can run `git commit --no-verify` or skip hooks entirely. Hooks are a developer convenience and a fast feedback loop - the **authoritative gate is CI**. Mirror every important hook check in the pipeline (see `/genskills:ci-fix`). 5. **Respect the escape hatch.** `--no-verify` exists for emergencies (hotfixes, reverting a bad hook, WIP commits). Document it, never try to defeat it, and make it obvious in failure output. A hook you cannot bypass is a hook people will rip out. 6. **One manager, committed to the repo.** Pick a single hook manager and commit its config so every clone gets identical hooks. Mixing managers, or relying on hand-edited `.git/hooks` that never leave a machine, guarantees drift. --- ## Process ### Step 0: Load Context

Apply safe fixes freely; apply unsafe fixes only after reading the change. 2. **Fix the root, never suppress.** A `// eslint-disable-next-line` or `# noqa` hides the symptom and rots over time. Suppress only when the rule is genuinely wrong for that exact line, and say why in a comment. 3. **Never mass-disable rules to make the build pass.** Disabling a rule project-wide to clear violations trades a visible problem for an invisible one. If a rule is consistently wrong for this codebase, surface it for the user to decide - don't silently edit the config. 4. **Linting and formatting are separate concerns.** A linter finds bugs and bad patterns; a formatter enforces a canonical style. Keep them from fighting - the formatter's output is authoritative for style, and the linter must defer (e.g. `eslint-config-prettier`). 5. **Respect the configured tool and its config.** Detect what the project already uses. Do not introduce a new linter, change `printWidth`, or "improve" the config as a side effect of a fix. 6. **Verify clean after fixing.** Re-run every tool in check mode (no `--fix`). A fix that leaves new violations isn't done. --- ## Process ### Step 0: Load Project Context

skills.name: genskills-type-check
skills.description: Run type checking and fix type errors in TypeScript, Python (mypy/pyright), or other typed languages. Triggers on: "type check", "fix types", "fix type errors", "tsc errors", "mypy errors". user-invocable: true argument-hint: "[file or directory] [--strict] [--install-types]" allowed-tools: "Read, Edit, Grep, Glob, Bash(npx tsc*), Bash(npm run*), Bash(npm install*), Bash(yarn *), Bash(pnpm *), Bash(mypy*), Bash(pyright*), Bash(pip install*), Bash(flow*), Bash(srb tc*), Bash(bundle exec srb*), Bash(cargo check*), Bash(go vet*), Bash(npm test*)" genskills-version: "1.5.0" genskills-category: "code-quality" genskills-depends: [] --- # Type Check Run type checking and resolve type errors at their root - turning a red type checker green by *strengthening* the code, never by silencing the tool. Types are a proof system; a passing check should mean the proof holds, not that the proof was deleted. ## Core Principles Internalize these before changing a single annotation: 1. **Fix the type, do not cast it.** A cast (`as`, `cast()`, `!`) tells the compiler to stop checking. The error does not go away - it moves to runtime. Narrow, annotate, or restructure instead. 2. **No `any`, no `# type: ignore` escape hatches.** `any` is contagious - it poisons every value it touches. Prefer `unknown` and narrow. Escape hatches are last resorts, scoped to one line, with a comment justifying them. 3. **A type error is often a real bug.** The checker found a path where the data is not what you assumed. Investigate the logic before "fixing the types" - you may be papering over a null deref or a wrong API contract. 4. **Strictness is progress, not punishment.** Each flag you turn on (`strictNullChecks`, `noUncheckedIndexedAccess`, mypy `strict`) removes a class of runtime errors permanently. Adopt strictness incrementally and never regress it. 5. **Types document intent.** A precise signature is the cheapest, most honest documentation in the codebase - it cannot drift out of date. Prefer the narrowest type that is true (literal unions, branded types, generics) over the widest one that compiles. 6. **Validate at the boundary, trust within.** Type assertions are earned by runtime validation at I/O edges (API responses, env vars, JSON, form input). Inside that boundary, the types are real and need no defensive casting. --- ## Process ### Step 0: Load Project Context

skills.name: genskills-refactor
skills.description: Intelligently refactor code to improve structure, readability, and maintainability while preserving behavior. Triggers on: "refactor", "clean up", "restructure", "simplify". user-invocable: true argument-hint: "[file or function] [--type extract|simplify|rename|dedup|modernize|decouple] [--scope minimal|moderate|aggressive]" allowed-tools: "Read, Edit, Grep, Glob, Bash(git diff*), Bash(git stash*), Bash(git status*), Bash(git log*), Bash(npm test*), Bash(npm run*), Bash(npx vitest*), Bash(npx jest*), Bash(npx tsc*), Bash(npx eslint*), Bash(pytest*), Bash(mypy*), Bash(ruff*), Bash(cargo test*), Bash(cargo check*), Bash(go test*), Bash(go vet*)" genskills-version: "1.5.0" genskills-category: "code-quality" genskills-depends: [] --- # Refactor A disciplined playbook for improving the internal structure of code without changing its external behavior. Refactoring is a sequence of small, behavior-preserving transformations validated by tests at every step - not a rewrite, and not an excuse to change what the code does. ## Core Principles Internalize these before touching any code: 1. **Behavior is sacred.** Refactoring changes structure, never observable behavior. Same inputs, same outputs, same side effects. If behavior changes, it is not a refactoring - it is a rewrite or a bug. 2. **Tests are the safety net.** Every refactoring is validated by a green test suite. No tests means no net - establish a baseline first, and if coverage is thin around the target, add characterization tests before you touch anything. 3. **Small steps, always green.** Take the smallest transformation that stands on its own, then re-run tests. A sequence of verified small steps is safe; one giant leap is a gamble. Commit (or stash a checkpoint) between steps so you can always retreat to green. 4. **One refactoring at a time.** Do not mix an extract with a rename with a modernize in a single edit. Separate transformations are reviewable, attributable, and individually revertible. 5. **Separate refactoring from feature work.** Never refactor and add functionality in the same commit. Reviewers cannot distinguish a structural move from a behavioral change when they are tangled together. 6. **Refactor to remove a smell, not to chase aesthetics.** Each change should pay down a concrete code smell or unblock upcoming work. "Cleaner" is not a goal; "easier to change safely" is. --- ## Process ### Step 0: Load Project Context

Triggers on: "architecture diagram", "generate diagram", "dependency graph", "system diagram", "data flow diagram", "codebase map". user-invocable: true argument-hint: "[type: system|dependency|data-flow|class|sequence|er|api|c4] [--scope <dir>] [--format mermaid|plantuml|d2]" allowed-tools: "Read, Write, Grep, Glob, Bash(npx madge*), Bash(npx depcruise*), Bash(npx mmdc*), Bash(git log*), Bash(git diff*)" genskills-version: "1.5.0" genskills-category: "documentation" genskills-depends: [] --- # Architecture Diagram Generate visual architecture diagrams from codebase analysis - diagrams that are derived from how the code actually works, versionable as text, and pitched at the right level of detail for their audience. ## Core Principles Before drawing anything, internalize these: 1. **Diagram-as-code.** Always emit text-based diagrams (Mermaid, PlantUML, D2) committed alongside the code - never binary images. Text diagrams diff in PRs, review like code, and render natively in GitHub. A `.png` rots silently; a `.mmd` shows up in `git blame`. 2. **Right altitude per audience.** Match the C4 level to the reader. A CEO wants Context (one box for your system); a new hire wants Containers; an engineer touching one service wants Components. Mixing altitudes in one diagram is the most common failure mode. 3. **Derive from real code structure.** Every node and edge must trace back to something concrete - an import, a route handler, an ORM relation, a service client, a queue topic. Do not draw the architecture you wish existed; draw the one the code describes. 4. **One concern per diagram.** A diagram answering "how does a request flow?" should not also try to show the deployment topology or the class hierarchy. Split into multiple focused diagrams rather than one unreadable everything-map. 5. **Keep in sync.** A diagram is only trustworthy if it tracks the code. Generate it from source, note the commit/date it was generated from, and regenerate on meaningful structural change. A stale diagram is worse than none - it actively misleads. 6. **Readable beats complete.** Past ~20 nodes a diagram stops communicating. Aggregate, group into subsystems, or split. Omitting detail for clarity is a feature, not a compromise. --- ## Process ### Step 0: Load Context

skills.name: genskills-doc-gen
skills.description: Generate documentation for code - inline comments, JSDoc/docstrings, module docs. Triggers on: "document this", "add docs", "generate documentation", "add comments", "write docstrings". user-invocable: true argument-hint: "[file or directory] [--style jsdoc|tsdoc|google|numpy|sphinx] [--scope public|all] [--update]" allowed-tools: "Read, Write, Edit, Grep, Glob, Bash(git diff*), Bash(git log*), Bash(npx typedoc*), Bash(npx jsdoc*), Bash(sphinx-build*), Bash(pdoc*), Bash(go doc*), Bash(cargo doc*), Bash(interrogate*)" genskills-version: "1.5.0" genskills-category: "documentation" genskills-depends: [] --- # Documentation Generator A disciplined approach to writing documentation that earns its place - inline comments, docstrings, JSDoc/TSDoc, module headers, and the conventions each language and doc generator expects. The goal is documentation a future reader thanks you for, not noise they learn to skim past. ## Core Principles Before writing a single comment, internalize these: 1. **Document *why*, not *what*.** The code already says what it does. Good docs explain intent, trade-offs, edge cases, and the non-obvious - why this caching strategy, why this order, why this exists at all. `/** Gets the user */` on `getUser()` is pure noise. 2. **Examples beat prose.** One copy-pasteable usage example teaches more than three paragraphs. For any non-trivial API, show the call. 3. **Keep docs next to the code.** Docstrings and doc comments live with the thing they describe so they are read - and updated - in the same edit. Out-of-band docs rot; co-located docs survive. 4. **Match the existing style exactly.** A codebase has one documentation voice, format, tense, and density. Detect it and conform. Introducing NumPy docstrings into a Google-style project is a regression even if each docstring is "better." 5. **Do not over-document the obvious.** Trivial getters, self-explanatory one-liners, and code whose name + signature already tell the whole story should be left alone. Every comment is a maintenance liability; only add ones that pay rent. 6. **Never let docs drift from code.** A wrong comment is worse than no comment. When updating, reconcile params, return types, and thrown errors with the actual signature. Only modify documentation - never the code itself. --- ## Process ### Step 0: Load Project Context

skills.name: genskills-readme-gen
skills.description: Generate or update a comprehensive README.md for the project. Triggers on: "generate README", "create README", "update README", "write README", "project README". user-invocable: true argument-hint: "[style: minimal|standard|detailed] [--update] [--badges] [--no-toc]" allowed-tools: "Read, Write, Edit, Grep, Glob, Bash(git remote*), Bash(git log*), Bash(npm run*), Bash(npm ls*), Bash(node *), Bash(cat *), Bash(gh repo*), Bash(npx markdown-link-check*)" genskills-version: "1.5.0" genskills-category: "documentation" genskills-depends: [] --- # README Generator Generate a README that a stranger can read in two minutes and a contributor can rely on for years - derived from what the project actually is, not from a generic template. ## Core Principles Internalize these before writing a single line: 1. **Lead with what + why, then the working quickstart.** The first screenful must answer "what is this and why should I care?" The second must get the reader from zero to a running example. Everything else is reference material that follows. 2. **Derive every claim from the repo.** Names, commands, versions, and examples come from `package.json`/`pyproject.toml`/source/tests - never from imagination. A README that documents a `start` script the project does not have is worse than no README. 3. **The quickstart must actually run.** Use the real package manager, the real entry point, the real env vars. Copy-paste-and-it-works is the bar. If you cannot verify a command, say so rather than inventing output. 4. **Structure for scanning, not reading.** Headings, tables, and code fences let a reader jump to the section they need. Prose walls hide information. A table of contents earns its place once the document exceeds ~5 sections. 5. **Keep badges and links honest.** A badge is a claim - only add a CI badge if CI exists, an npm badge if it is published, a coverage badge if coverage is measured. Broken or aspirational badges destroy trust faster than missing ones. 6. **Match depth to the project type.** A library README is API-first; a CLI README is command-first; an app README is setup-first. Do not force one shape onto another (see Project-Type Playbooks). --- ## Process ### Step 0: Load Project Context
